narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like coming from an upper-middle-class family and planning to attend full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 52.5%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your LSAT score of 32, which stands well below the typical student's average of about 37 and was the single biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, though lower than the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor, helping to pull the prediction toward success. Similarly, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4 provided some counterbalance, suggesting solid performance relative to peers and nudging the odds toward passing. Your family income level and Year 1 decile of 4 had smaller influences tilting slightly toward higher risk, while your full-time status offered a minor boost. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who often have stronger early academic deciles around 5-6, higher GPAs, and LSATs in the mid-to-high 30s—your profile highlighted these gaps as the key concerns for bar passage. We know this outcome can feel disheartening, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive academic pipelines. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average, bolstering your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses if possible, and highlighting any additional experiences that demonstrate resilience. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting schools with supportive programs for underrepresented groups. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing forward.
